preamble: The transformation from the voiceless velar fricative /x/ to the palatal glide /j/ is a common form of palatalization and lenition. When /x/ appears before a front vowel, its place of articulation naturally shifts forward to the palatal region (becoming [ç]). This palatal fricative can then weaken further, losing its frication to become the glide /j/.

examples.note: The allophone [ç] of /x/ before front vowels is a precursor to further changes.
While Old Spanish had /ʃ/, modern Spanish has /x/, but the reverse path shows how palatals and velars interact. A shift from /x/ to /j/ would be a fronting and weakening process.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
